---
title: "4.14 Linear Search"
subtitle: "Searching Algorithms"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Searching Algorithms

### Linear and binary search

Computers store vast amounts of data.

One strength of computers is their ability to find things quickly. This ability is called **searching**.

For the AP CSA exam you need to know both **linear (sequential) search** and **binary search** algorithms.

If binary search requires the values in an array or list to be sorted, sorting algorithms will be covered in the next lesson.

## Linear Search

### Unsorted data

Linear or Sequential search can be used to find a value in **unsorted** data.

It usually starts at the first element and walks through the array or `ArrayList` until it finds the value and returns its index.

If it reaches the end without finding the value, the search method usually returns `-1`.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: seqSearch`

The code for `sequentialSearch` for arrays below is from a previous AP CSA course description.

## ArraySearcher Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class ArraySearcher
{
    public static int sequentialSearch(int[] elements, int target)
    {
        for (int j = 0; j < elements.length; j++)
        {
            if (elements[j] == target)
            {
                return j;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[] numArray = {3, -2, 9, 38, -23};
        System.out.println("Tests of sequentialSearch");
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numArray, 3));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numArray, 9));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numArray, -23));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numArray, 99));
    }
}
```
:::

## seqSearch Test Target

### Runestone expected output

```text
Tests of sequentialSearch
0
2
4
-1
```

## ArrayList Linear Search

### `size()` and `get(i)`

The same algorithms can be used with arrays or `ArrayList` objects.

With `ArrayList`, use `size()` and `get(i)` instead of `length` and `[i]`.

There are methods such as `contains` that can be used in `ArrayList`, but they are not in the AP CSA Java subset.

## ArrayListSearcher Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.util.*;

public class ArrayListSearcher
{
    public static int sequentialSearch(ArrayList<Integer> elements, int target)
    {
        for (int j = 0; j < elements.size(); j++)
        {
            if (elements.get(j) == target)
            {
                return j;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        ArrayList<Integer> numList = new ArrayList<Integer>();
        numList.add(3);
        numList.add(-2);
        numList.add(9);
        numList.add(38);
        numList.add(-23);
        System.out.println("Tests of sequentialSearch");
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numList, 3));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numList, 9));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numList, -23));
        System.out.println(sequentialSearch(numList, 99));
    }
}
```
:::

## Quick Checks

### `qss_1` and `qss_2`

Which causes the **longest** execution of a sequential search looking for a value in an array of integers?

Correct answer: **The value is not in the array.**

Which causes the **shortest** execution?

Correct answer: **The value is the first one in the array.**

## String Linear Search

### Use `equals`, not `==`

You can also look for a `String` in an array or list, but use `equals` rather than `==`.

`==` is only true when the two references refer to the same `String` object.

`equals` returns true if the characters in the two `String` objects are the same.

## SearchTest Source Code

```java
public class SearchTest
{
    public static int sequentialSearch(String[] elements, String target)
    {
        for (int j = 0; j < elements.length; j++)
        {
            if (elements[j].equals(target))
            {
                return j;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        String[] arr1 = {"blue", "red", "purple", "green"};
        int index = sequentialSearch(arr1, "red");
        System.out.println(index);
        index = sequentialSearch(arr1, "pink");
        System.out.println(index);
    }
}
```

Expected output:

```text
1
-1
```

## Linear Search with 2D Arrays

### Search each row

We can apply the linear search algorithm to data in a 2D array.

Loop through each row of the 2D array and apply the linear search algorithm to each row to find an element.

The source asks you to change the code to work with a 2D array of `String` objects and to use `equals` to compare strings.

## Search Source Code

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
public class Search
{
    public static boolean search(int[][] array, int value)
    {
        boolean found = false;
        for (int row = 0; row < array.length; row++)
        {
            for (int col = 0; col < array[0].length; col++)
            {
                if (array[row][col] == value)
                {
                     found = true;
                }
            }
        }
        return found;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        int[][] matrix = { {3, 2, 3}, {4, 3, 6}, {8, 9, 3}, {10, 3, 3}};
        System.out.println(search(matrix, 10));
        System.out.println(search(matrix, 11));

        // Comment out the code above, and try these:
        // String[][] matrix2 = { {"a","b","c"},{"d","e","f"} };
        // System.out.println(search(matrix2, "b"));
    }
}
```
:::

## 2D Search Test Target

### Runestone extra test

The Runestone test calls `search` with a `String[][]` and `"b"`.

Expected return value:

```text
true
```

The modified method must therefore use string comparison correctly.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

- return the index when linear search finds the target
- return `-1` after the loop if the target is not found
- use `size()` and `get(i)` for `ArrayList`
- use `equals` for `String` search
- search a 2D array by traversing rows and columns

## End

### 4.14 Linear Search

Next: Binary Search.
